core_meaning: <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> means 'place' or 'spot' in concrete use, but more importantly it works as a grammar word indicating a point in time, a situation, or a relevant aspect — as in 'just about to', 'in the middle of', or 'the thing about X is…'

sentences.jp: ちょうどいい<ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>来<rt>き</rt></ruby>てくれた！
sentences.translation: You came at just the right time!
sentences.notes: <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> here refers to a point in time ('just the right moment'), not a physical place — one of its most common everyday uses.

sentences.jp: これから<ruby>出<rt>で</rt></ruby>かけるところだったのに、<ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>り<ruby>出<rt>だ</rt></ruby>した。
sentences.translation: I was just about to head out when it started raining.
sentences.notes: 〜る<ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby>だった expresses 'was just on the verge of doing' — a key grammatical pattern to learn alongside this word.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>のいいところは、いつも<ruby>正直<rt>しょうじき</rt></ruby>なことだと<ruby>思<rt>おも</rt></ruby>う。
sentences.translation: I think her best quality is that she's always honest.
sentences.notes: <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> used as 'aspect' or 'quality' — a very natural collocation when talking about a person's traits.

sentences.jp: <ruby>駅<rt>えき</rt></ruby>から<ruby>近<rt>ちか</rt></ruby>い<ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>住<rt>す</rt></ruby>みたいんだよね。
sentences.translation: I want to live somewhere close to the station.
sentences.notes: Straightforward use as a physical or general 'place' — the most literal meaning, common in everyday speech.

sentences.jp: <ruby>報告書<rt>ほうこくしょ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>確認<rt>かくにん</rt></ruby>していたところ、いくつかの<ruby>誤<rt>あやま</rt></ruby>りが<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>つかりました。
sentences.translation: While I was reviewing the report, I found several errors.
sentences.notes: 〜ていた<ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> in formal/written contexts expresses 'while doing' or 'in the course of doing' — common in business and official writing.

dont_use: <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> should not be used for all kinds of 'places' interchangeably — for specific facilities or institutions (hospital, school, restaurant), use <ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby> or just name the place directly. Don't overuse it as a vague 'place' substitute when a more concrete noun fits better. Also, learners often forget the grammatical uses entirely and treat it as only a physical noun — the temporal and aspectual uses (〜るところ、〜たところ) are just as important.

confused_with.contrast: A common mistake is saying 「いい<ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>っている？」when asking 'Do you know a good spot?' — this is actually fine, but learners sometimes reach for <ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby> even in contexts where <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> is more natural, and vice versa. <ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby> focuses specifically on a physical, locatable space (e.g., 「<ruby>駐車場<rt>ちゅうしゃじょう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby>」— 'the location of the parking lot'), while <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> is broader and also carries the grammatical uses <ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby> cannot. You cannot replace <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> with <ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby> in any grammatical construction like 〜るところだ.

frequency: Extremely common across all registers — both as a concrete noun in everyday speech and as a grammar word in casual, standard, and formal Japanese. The grammatical uses appear constantly in natural speech and writing. One of the most versatile and high-frequency words in the language.
anki_hint: <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> isn't just a 'place' — when it follows a verb form (〜る、〜ている、〜た), it marks a point or phase in time, so watch for the verb form before it to know which meaning you're dealing with.
